raw: {"weights":{"R1.1":0.7,"R1.2":0.7,"R1.3":0.7,"R1.4":0.3,"R1.5":0.0,"R1.6":0.3,"R1.7":0.3,"RX.1":1.0,"RX.2":1.0,"RX.3":1.0,"RX.4":1.0,"RX.5":1.0},"active_set":["R1.1","R1.2","R1.3","RX.1","RX.2","RX.3","RX.4","RX.5"],"rationale":{"R1.1":"The artifact advances a specific contribution claim (fully inductive selective GNN with a learned abstention head); any general 'check' warrants verifying that the thesis, gap statement, and contribution are internally coherent and accurately stated.","R1.2":"The artifact contains an apparent load-bearing logical contradiction: it simultaneously asserts that SelectGNN is 'fully inductive' (no re-optimization on new nodes) yet trains a dedicated embedding vector e_v for every node v in the input graph — per-node learned embeddings are inherently transductive and cannot be computed for nodes unseen at training time. Logical consistency is central to virtually any reading of 'check this.'","R1.3":"A quantitative claim (6.3% selective-accuracy improvement over GraphSAGE-Abstain at 80% coverage) appears with no supporting table, figure, or experimental detail in the excerpt; verifying that claims are grounded in evidence is broadly implied by 'check this.'"},"clarification_needed":true,"clarification_question":"Should I prioritize a technical/logical review — checking internal consistency of claims, in particular the inductive-design assertion versus the per-node learned embeddings — or a writing-quality review focused on clarity, grammar, and prose polish?"}

rationale.R1.1: The artifact advances a specific contribution claim (fully inductive selective GNN with a learned abstention head); any general 'check' warrants verifying that the thesis, gap statement, and contribution are internally coherent and accurately stated.
rationale.R1.2: The artifact contains an apparent load-bearing logical contradiction: it simultaneously asserts that SelectGNN is 'fully inductive' (no re-optimization on new nodes) yet trains a dedicated embedding vector e_v for every node v in the input graph — per-node learned embeddings are inherently transductive and cannot be computed for nodes unseen at training time. Logical consistency is central to virtually any reading of 'check this.'
rationale.R1.3: A quantitative claim (6.3% selective-accuracy improvement over GraphSAGE-Abstain at 80% coverage) appears with no supporting table, figure, or experimental detail in the excerpt; verifying that claims are grounded in evidence is broadly implied by 'check this.'

parsed.clarification_question: Should I prioritize a technical/logical review — checking internal consistency of claims, in particular the inductive-design assertion versus the per-node learned embeddings — or a writing-quality review focused on clarity, grammar, and prose polish?
